challenges.description:

challenges.hint: Map each char: if it is a letter, shift it within its case range using modulo 26. Convert to/from u8 for arithmetic.

steps.title: Classify each character
steps.explanation: Use is_ascii_lowercase() and is_ascii_uppercase() to determine if a character should be shifted. Non-letter characters pass through unchanged.
steps.code: if c.is_ascii_lowercase() { /* shift */ } else if c.is_ascii_uppercase() { /* shift */ } else { c }

steps.title: Apply modular shift
steps.explanation: Subtract the base (b'a' or b'A'), add the shift, take modulo 26 to wrap around the alphabet, then add the base back. Cast between u8 and char for arithmetic.
steps.code: (b'a' + (c as u8 - b'a' + shift) % 26) as char

Expected output:
Found 7 at index 3
Value 4 not found
challenges.hint: Maintain low and high bounds. Compare the middle element to the target and narrow the search half each iteration. Use usize carefully to avoid underflow.

steps.title: Set up bounds with exclusive high
steps.explanation: Use an exclusive upper bound (high = arr.len()) so the search range is [low, high). This avoids usize underflow since high never goes below 0.
steps.code:
let mut low = 0usize;
let mut high = arr.len();

steps.title: Narrow the search range
steps.explanation: Compute mid = low + (high - low) / 2 to prevent overflow. If arr[mid] < target, search the right half by setting low = mid + 1. If arr[mid] > target, search the left half by setting high = mid.

steps.title: Return None if not found
steps.explanation: If the loop exits without returning, the target is not in the array. Returning Option<usize> is idiomatic Rust for searches that may fail.

challenges.title: Fib Memo
challenges.description:
Write `fibonacci(n: u32) -> Vec<u64>` that returns the first `n` Fibonacci numbers using iterative memoization (store results in a Vec). fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1. Example: `fibonacci(8)` returns `[0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13]`.

Expected output:
[0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13]
challenges.hint: Build a Vec, seeding with 0 and 1. Each subsequent value is the sum of the two preceding values in the Vec.

steps.title: Seed base cases
steps.explanation: Handle the edge case of n == 0 by returning an empty vec. For i == 0, push 0; for i == 1, push 1. These are the Fibonacci base cases that every subsequent value depends on.
steps.code: if i == 0 { fibs.push(0); } else if i == 1 { fibs.push(1); }

steps.title: Build iteratively with memoization
steps.explanation: For each index i >= 2, the value is fibs[i-1] + fibs[i-2]. Because previous results are already stored in the Vec, this is O(n) time and O(n) space — much better than naive recursion which is O(2^n).
steps.code: fibs.push(fibs[i - 1] + fibs[i - 2]);

steps.title: Pre-allocate with capacity
steps.explanation: Using Vec::with_capacity(n) avoids repeated memory reallocations as the Vec grows. This is a common Rust optimization when the final size is known.

Expected output:
true
false
challenges.hint: Normalize both strings to lowercase, filter out spaces, sort the characters into Vecs, and compare.

steps.title: Create a normalization closure
steps.explanation: Define a closure that converts a string to a sorted Vec<char> after lowercasing and filtering spaces. Closures in Rust can capture nothing and act like local functions — perfect for reusable transformations.

steps.title: Compare normalized forms
steps.explanation: Two strings are anagrams if and only if their sorted character sequences are identical. Rust's Vec<char> supports == comparison, making the final check a simple equality test.
steps.code: normalize(a) == normalize(b)

challenges.title: Spiral Order
challenges.description:
Write `spiral_order(matrix: &[Vec<i32>]) -> Vec<i32>` that returns elements of a matrix in spiral (clockwise) order, starting from the top-left. Example: for `[[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]]`, the result is `[1,2,3,6,9,8,7,4,5]`.

Expected output:
[1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 8, 7, 4, 5]
challenges.hint: Maintain four boundaries: top, bottom, left, right. Traverse the top row, right column, bottom row, left column, then shrink boundaries inward. Repeat until all elements are visited.

steps.title: Define four boundaries
steps.explanation: Use top, bottom, left, right as i32 values to track which rows and columns haven't been visited yet. Using i32 instead of usize allows safe decrement without underflow panics.

steps.title: Traverse four edges per layer
steps.explanation: In each iteration: traverse the top row left-to-right, then the right column top-to-bottom, then the bottom row right-to-left (if rows remain), then the left column bottom-to-top (if columns remain). After each traversal, shrink the corresponding boundary.

steps.title: Guard against double-counting
steps.explanation: Before traversing the bottom row and left column, check that the boundaries haven't crossed (top <= bottom, left <= right). Without these guards, single-row or single-column matrices would produce duplicate entries.

challenges.title: Two Sum
challenges.description:
Write `two_sum(nums: &[i32], target: i32) -> Option<(usize, usize)>` that finds two indices whose values add up to `target`. Return the first pair found (smaller index first). Example: `two_sum(&[2,7,11,15], 9)` returns `Some((0, 1))`.

Expected output:
(0, 1)
No pair found
challenges.hint: Use a HashMap to store each number's index as you iterate. For each element, check if (target - current) is already in the map.

steps.title: Use a HashMap for O(1) lookups
steps.explanation: Create a HashMap<i32, usize> that maps each seen number to its index. This lets you check in constant time whether the complement (target - current number) has already been encountered.
steps.code: let mut seen: HashMap<i32, usize> = HashMap::new();

steps.title: Check complement before inserting
steps.explanation: For each element, compute the complement and check if it's in the map. If found, return both indices. If not, insert the current number. This guarantees the smaller index is returned first and runs in O(n) time.

steps.title: Pattern match the result
steps.explanation: Using Option<(usize, usize)> as the return type is idiomatic Rust. The caller uses match or if let to handle both the found and not-found cases cleanly.

challenges.title: RLE Encode
challenges.description:
Write `rle_encode(s: &str) -> String` that performs run-length encoding. Consecutive identical characters are replaced by the character followed by its count. Example: `rle_encode("aaabccdddd")` returns `"a3b1c2d4"`.

Expected output:
a3b1c2d4
A1B1C1
challenges.hint: Iterate through characters, tracking the current character and its count. When the character changes (or the string ends), append the character and count to the result.

steps.title: Collect chars for indexed access
steps.explanation: Rust strings are UTF-8 and can't be indexed directly. Collecting into Vec<char> gives O(1) random access, which simplifies comparing adjacent characters.
steps.code: let chars: Vec<char> = s.chars().collect();

steps.title: Track runs of identical characters
steps.explanation: Iterate from index 1, comparing each character to the previous one. Increment a counter while they match. When they differ, flush the previous character and its count to the result string, then reset the counter.

steps.title: Flush the last run
steps.explanation: After the loop, the last run hasn't been flushed yet. Always push the final character and count. This is a common pattern in accumulation loops.

challenges.title: Merge Sorted
challenges.description:
Write `merge_sorted(a: &[i32], b: &[i32]) -> Vec<i32>` that merges two sorted slices into one sorted Vec. Example: `merge_sorted(&[1,3,5], &[2,4,6])` returns `[1,2,3,4,5,6]`.

Expected output:
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
[1, 2, 3]
challenges.hint: Use two index pointers, one for each slice. Compare current elements and push the smaller one. After one slice is exhausted, append the rest of the other.

steps.title: Two-pointer merge
steps.explanation: Initialize two index pointers at 0. Compare a[i] and b[j], push the smaller element, and advance that pointer. This runs in O(n + m) time — the same merge step used in merge sort.

steps.title: Append remaining elements
steps.explanation: After the loop, one slice may still have elements. Use extend_from_slice with a range to efficiently append the remainder. Rust slices make this a zero-copy operation on the reference.
steps.code:
result.extend_from_slice(&a[i..]);
result.extend_from_slice(&b[j..]);

steps.title: Pre-allocate capacity
steps.explanation: Vec::with_capacity(a.len() + b.len()) allocates enough memory upfront, avoiding reallocations during the merge. This is a good habit when the final size is known.

challenges.title: String Compress
challenges.description:
Write `compress(s: &str) -> String` that compresses a string by replacing runs of characters with the character and count. Unlike basic RLE, single-character runs keep the count. Example: `compress("aabccc")` returns `"a2b1c3"`.

Expected output:
a2b1c3
x1y1z1
challenges.hint: Use iterators: peekable lets you look ahead to determine run length. Or use a fold/scan approach to accumulate runs.

steps.title: Use a peekable iterator
steps.explanation: Calling .peekable() on a char iterator lets you look at the next element without consuming it. This is perfect for counting consecutive runs — peek ahead to see if the next character matches the current one.
steps.code: let mut chars = s.chars().peekable();

steps.title: Count each run
steps.explanation: For each character, enter an inner loop that increments a counter while peek() returns the same character. When the next character differs (or the iterator ends), append the character and its count to the result.

steps.title: Peekable vs indexing
steps.explanation: Using Peekable<Chars> is more idiomatic and efficient than collecting into a Vec<char> for indexed access. It works directly on the character stream without extra allocation.

challenges.title: Bracket Check
challenges.description:
Write `is_valid_brackets(s: &str) -> bool` that checks if a string of brackets `()[]{}` is properly nested and matched. Example: `is_valid_brackets("({[]})")` returns `true`, `is_valid_brackets("([)]")` returns `false`.

Expected output:
true
false
true
challenges.hint: Use a Vec as a stack. Push opening brackets. On a closing bracket, pop and check that it matches the corresponding opener. The string is valid if the stack is empty at the end.

steps.title: Use a Vec as a stack
steps.explanation: Vec<char> with push() and pop() makes a natural stack in Rust. Pop returns Option<char>, which makes checking for mismatches elegant — if pop() returns None or the wrong bracket, it's invalid.
steps.code: let mut stack: Vec<char> = Vec::new();

steps.title: Match brackets with pattern matching
steps.explanation: Rust's match expression cleanly handles all bracket types. Opening brackets are pushed. For each closing bracket, pop the stack and check the opener matches. The wildcard _ ignores non-bracket characters.
steps.code:
match c {
    '(' | '[' | '{' => stack.push(c),
    ')' => if stack.pop() != Some('(') { return false; },
    ']' => if stack.pop() != Some('[') { return false; },
    '}' => if stack.pop() != Some('{') { return false; },
    _ => {},
}

steps.title: Final empty-stack check
steps.explanation: After processing all characters, the stack must be empty. If opening brackets remain, they were never closed. An empty input string is considered valid.

challenges.title: Pair Finder
challenges.description:
Write `find_pairs(nums: &[i32], target: i32) -> Vec<(i32, i32)>` that returns all unique pairs of values (sorted, smaller first) that sum to `target`. No duplicate pairs. Example: `find_pairs(&[1,2,3,4,5,6], 7)` returns `[(1,6),(2,5),(3,4)]`.

Expected output:
[(1, 6), (2, 5), (3, 4)]
[(3, 7)]
challenges.hint: Use a HashSet for O(1) lookups. For each number, check if (target - num) is in the set. Track used pairs to avoid duplicates. Always put the smaller value first.

steps.title: Track seen values and used pairs
steps.explanation: Use two HashSets: 'seen' stores values encountered so far, 'used' stores pairs already added to results. This prevents duplicate pairs when the same combination appears multiple times.
steps.code:
let mut seen = HashSet::new();
let mut used = HashSet::new();

steps.title: Normalize pair ordering
steps.explanation: Always store pairs as (min, max) to ensure consistent ordering. Rust's i32::min() and i32::max() make this concise. This normalization is key to deduplication.
steps.code: let pair = (num.min(complement), num.max(complement));

steps.title: Sort and return
steps.explanation: Sorting the result gives deterministic output. Tuples implement Ord in Rust, so Vec<(i32,i32)> can be sorted directly — first by the first element, then by the second.

challenges.title: Common Prefix
challenges.description:
Write `longest_common_prefix(strs: &[&str]) -> String` that returns the longest common prefix of a slice of strings. Example: `longest_common_prefix(&["flower","flow","flight"])` returns `"fl"`.

Expected output:
fl

rust
challenges.hint: Start with the first string as the prefix. For each subsequent string, shorten the prefix until it matches the start of that string.

steps.title: Start with the first string
steps.explanation: Use the first string as the initial prefix candidate. The longest common prefix can never be longer than the shortest string, and starting with any string naturally bounds the search.
steps.code: let mut prefix = strs[0].to_string();

steps.title: Shrink prefix until it matches
steps.explanation: For each subsequent string, use starts_with() to check if it begins with the current prefix. If not, pop() the last character and try again. This progressively shortens the prefix until it's common to all strings.

steps.title: Handle edge cases
steps.explanation: An empty input slice returns an empty string. A single-element slice returns that string itself. The pop() + is_empty() guard handles the case where strings share no common prefix at all.

challenges.title: Custom Iterator
challenges.description:
Define a struct `StepRange { current: i32, end: i32, step: i32 }` and implement the `Iterator` trait for it, yielding values from `current` up to (but not including) `end`, incrementing by `step`. Example: `StepRange { current: 0, end: 10, step: 3 }` yields `0, 3, 6, 9`.

Expected output:
0
3
6
9
Sum: 18
challenges.hint: In next(), check if current < end. If so, save the current value, advance by step, and return Some(saved). Otherwise return None.

steps.title: Define the struct state
steps.explanation: StepRange holds three fields: current (the next value to yield), end (the exclusive upper bound), and step (the increment). The struct itself is the iterator's mutable state.

steps.title: Implement the Iterator trait
steps.explanation: Set type Item = i32 to declare what the iterator yields. In next(), check if current < end. If yes, save the current value, advance current by step, and return Some(val). Otherwise, return None to signal the iterator is exhausted.

steps.title: Leverage Iterator combinators for free
steps.explanation: By implementing Iterator, you get .sum(), .collect(), .map(), .filter(), and dozens of other methods automatically. The for loop also works because IntoIterator is auto-implemented for any Iterator.

challenges.description: Implement generic `Stack<T>` with methods `new`, `push`, `pop`, `peek`, using `Vec<T>` as storage.
challenges.hint: Delegate behavior to Vec push/pop/last.

challenges.title: LRU Cache
challenges.description:
Implement a Least Recently Used (LRU) cache with a fixed capacity using a HashMap and VecDeque. Implement `LruCache::new(capacity)`, `get(&mut self, key: i32) -> Option<i32>`, and `put(&mut self, key: i32, value: i32)`. When the cache exceeds capacity, evict the least recently used entry. In main, create a cache with capacity 2, then: put(1,10), put(2,20), print get(1) -> Some(10), put(3,30) which evicts key 2, print get(2) -> None, print get(3) -> Some(30).

Expected output:
Some(10)
None
Some(30)
challenges.hint: When accessing or inserting a key, remove it from the VecDeque and push it to the back so the front always holds the least recently used key.

steps.title: Design the LRU data structure
steps.explanation: Use a HashMap for O(1) key-value lookups and a VecDeque to track access order. The front of the VecDeque holds the least recently used key, the back holds the most recent.

steps.title: Implement get with recency update
steps.explanation: On get, if the key exists, remove it from VecDeque and push it to the back to mark it as most recently used. Return None if the key is absent.

steps.title: Implement put with eviction
steps.explanation: On put, if the key already exists, update it and refresh its position. If at capacity, evict the front of the VecDeque (least recently used) and remove it from the HashMap before inserting the new entry.

challenges.title: Trie Search
challenges.description:
Implement a Trie (prefix tree) with `insert(&mut self, word: &str)` and `search(&self, word: &str) -> bool`. Use a HashMap<char, TrieNode> for children and a bool to mark end-of-word. In main, insert "apple" and "app", then search for "apple" (true), "app" (true), "ap" (false), "orange" (false). Print each result on its own line.

Expected output:
true
true
false
false
challenges.hint: For insert, iterate over characters creating child nodes as needed. For search, walk the trie char by char and check is_end at the final node.

steps.title: Model the Trie nodes
steps.explanation: Each TrieNode has a HashMap mapping characters to child nodes and a boolean is_end to mark whether a complete word ends at this node. The Trie struct holds the root node.

steps.title: Implement insert
steps.explanation: Walk through each character of the word, creating a new child TrieNode via entry().or_insert() if it doesn't exist. Mark the last node's is_end as true.

steps.title: Implement search
steps.explanation: Traverse the trie character by character. If any character is missing, return false. If all characters are found, return the is_end flag of the final node to distinguish complete words from prefixes.

challenges.title: Bound Extremes
challenges.description:
Write a generic function `min_max<T: PartialOrd + Clone>(items: &[T]) -> Option<(T, T)>` that returns the minimum and maximum of a slice in a single pass. Return None for empty slices. In main, call it with `[3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]` and print the result, then call it on an empty i32 slice and print.

Expected output:
Some((1, 9))
None
challenges.hint: Initialize min and max from the first element, then iterate over the rest comparing with PartialOrd.

steps.title: Use trait bounds for generics
steps.explanation: The function signature `<T: PartialOrd + Clone>` ensures T can be compared and cloned. This allows the function to work with any type that supports ordering, not just integers.
steps.code: fn min_max<T: PartialOrd + Clone>(items: &[T]) -> Option<(T, T)>

steps.title: Handle the empty case with ?
steps.explanation: Use items.first()? to return None immediately for empty slices. The ? operator on Option is idiomatic Rust for early returns.
steps.code: let first = items.first()?.clone();

steps.title: Single-pass min/max scan
steps.explanation: Initialize min and max from the first element, then iterate through the rest. Compare each item against both min and max, updating via clone when a new extreme is found.

challenges.title: Rising Sequence
challenges.description:
Write `lis_length(nums: &[i32]) -> usize` to compute the length of the longest increasing subsequence using dynamic programming with O(n^2) approach. For `[10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18]` the LIS is `[2, 3, 7, 101]` with length 4. For `[0, 1, 0, 3, 2, 3]` the answer is 4. Print both results.

Expected output:
4
4
challenges.hint: Create a dp array where dp[i] = length of LIS ending at index i. For each i, check all j < i where nums[j] < nums[i] and take dp[j] + 1. Answer is the max of the dp array.

steps.title: Set up the DP table
steps.explanation: Create a dp vector of length n, all initialized to 1, because the minimum LIS ending at any element is the element itself (length 1).
steps.code: let mut dp = vec![1usize; n];

steps.title: Fill the DP table with nested loops
steps.explanation: For each index i from 1..n, check all previous indices j. If nums[j] < nums[i], then we can extend the subsequence ending at j, so dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). This is the classic O(n^2) DP approach.

steps.title: Return the maximum
steps.explanation: The answer is the maximum value in the dp array, since the LIS could end at any position.
steps.code: *dp.iter().max().unwrap()

challenges.title: Graph Shortest
challenges.description:
Implement BFS shortest path on an unweighted directed graph using an adjacency list. Write `bfs_shortest(adj: &Vec<Vec<usize>>, start: usize, end: usize) -> Option<usize>` returning the shortest distance. Build a graph with 6 nodes and edges: 0->1, 0->2, 1->3, 2->3, 3->4, 4->5, 2->5. Find shortest path from 0 to 5 (length 3) and from 0 to 6 which doesn't exist (None).

Expected output:
Some(3)
None
challenges.hint: Use a VecDeque as a queue of (node, distance) pairs. Track visited nodes with a Vec<bool>. Return Some(distance) when you reach the end node.

steps.title: Handle edge cases
steps.explanation: Check if start equals end (distance 0) and if the indices are within the graph bounds. Return None for out-of-bound targets.

steps.title: BFS with distance tracking
steps.explanation: Use a VecDeque as a FIFO queue, pushing (node, distance) pairs. Mark nodes visited when enqueued to prevent revisiting. BFS guarantees the first time we reach the end node, it's via the shortest path.

steps.title: Return None if unreachable
steps.explanation: If the BFS completes without reaching the end node, the target is not reachable from start, so return None.

challenges.title: Matrix Chain
challenges.description:
Write `matrix_chain_order(dims: &[usize]) -> usize` implementing the matrix chain multiplication DP algorithm. Given dimensions array where matrix i has dimensions dims[i-1] x dims[i], find the minimum number of scalar multiplications. For dims = [10, 30, 5, 60], there are 3 matrices (10x30, 30x5, 5x60) and the optimal cost is 4500. For dims = [40, 20, 30, 10, 30], the optimal cost is 26000.

Expected output:
4500
26000
challenges.hint: Use a 2D DP table m[i][j] = minimum cost to multiply matrices i..j. Fill diagonally by chain length. For each split point k, cost = m[i][k] + m[k+1][j] + dims[i-1]*dims[k]*dims[j].

steps.title: Understand the DP table
steps.explanation: m[i][j] stores the minimum cost to multiply matrices from index i to j. Single matrices (chain length 1) have zero cost. We fill the table by increasing chain length.
steps.code: let mut m = vec![vec![0usize; n]; n];

steps.title: Iterate by chain length and split points
steps.explanation: For each chain length from 2 to n, and each starting index i, compute all possible split points k. The cost of splitting at k is: cost of left subchain + cost of right subchain + cost of multiplying the two resulting matrices.

steps.title: Return the optimal cost
steps.explanation: The answer is m[0][n-1], which represents the minimum cost to multiply the entire chain of matrices from the first to the last.

challenges.title: Trait Convert
challenges.description:
Define a `Celsius(f64)` struct and a `Fahrenheit(f64)` struct. Implement `From<Celsius> for Fahrenheit` using the formula F = C * 9/5 + 32, and `From<Fahrenheit> for Celsius` using C = (F - 32) * 5/9. In main, convert 100.0 Celsius to Fahrenheit and print it, then convert 32.0 Fahrenheit to Celsius and print it. Use the `.into()` syntax to demonstrate the Into trait auto-derivation.

Expected output:
212
0
challenges.hint: Implement From<Celsius> for Fahrenheit with Fahrenheit(c.0 * 9.0/5.0 + 32.0). Rust auto-derives Into when you implement From.

steps.title: Define newtype structs
steps.explanation: Celsius(f64) and Fahrenheit(f64) are tuple structs (newtypes). They wrap f64 to create distinct types that the compiler treats as different, enabling type-safe conversions.
steps.code:
struct Celsius(f64);
struct Fahrenheit(f64);

steps.title: Implement the From trait
steps.explanation: Rust's From trait defines how to create one type from another. By implementing From<Celsius> for Fahrenheit, Rust automatically provides Into<Fahrenheit> for Celsius. This is idiomatic Rust: always implement From, not Into.

steps.title: Use .into() for ergonomic conversion
steps.explanation: In main, `Celsius(100.0).into()` works because Rust auto-derives Into from our From implementation. The type annotation on the variable tells the compiler which Into conversion to use.
steps.code: let boiling: Fahrenheit = Celsius(100.0).into();

challenges.title: Config Builder
challenges.description:
Implement the Builder pattern for a `Config` struct with fields: host (String), port (u16), max_retries (u8). Create a `ConfigBuilder` with methods `host()`, `port()`, `max_retries()` that return `&mut Self` for chaining, and `build()` returning `Result<Config, String>`. host is required (error if empty), port defaults to 8080, max_retries defaults to 3. In main, build a valid config and print it, then try building with empty host and print the error.

Expected output:
Config { host: "localhost", port: 3000, max_retries: 5 }
Error: host is required
challenges.hint: Store host as Option<String> in the builder. In build(), check if host is Some and non-empty, otherwise return Err. Use default values for port and max_retries.

steps.title: Define Config and ConfigBuilder
steps.explanation: The Config struct holds the final values. The ConfigBuilder uses Option<String> for host (to detect if it was set) and plain values with defaults for port and max_retries.

steps.title: Chain setter methods with &mut Self
steps.explanation: Each setter takes &mut self and returns &mut Self, enabling method chaining like builder.host("x").port(3000). This is the standard Rust builder pattern.

steps.title: Validate in build()
steps.explanation: The build method validates required fields and returns Result. Using match with a guard clause (Some(h) if !h.is_empty()) elegantly handles both None and empty string cases.

challenges.title: Median Merge
challenges.description:
Write `find_median_sorted(a: &[i32], b: &[i32]) -> f64` that finds the median of two sorted arrays. Use a merge approach: merge both arrays into one sorted array, then compute the median. For a=[1,3] and b=[2], the median is 2.0. For a=[1,2] and b=[3,4], the median is 2.5.

Expected output:
2
2.5
challenges.hint: Merge both arrays into a single sorted vector using two pointers. Then if the total length is odd, return the middle element; if even, return the average of the two middle elements.

steps.title: Merge two sorted arrays
steps.explanation: Use two pointers i and j to merge the sorted arrays into one. Compare elements and push the smaller one. After one array is exhausted, append the remainder of the other.

steps.title: Compute the median
steps.explanation: For odd-length merged array, the median is the middle element. For even length, it's the average of the two middle elements. Integer division n/2 gives the correct index.

challenges.title: Cycle Detect
challenges.description:
Write `has_cycle(adj: &Vec<Vec<usize>>, n: usize) -> bool` that detects if a directed graph has a cycle using DFS with three-color marking (white=unvisited, gray=in-progress, black=done). A back edge to a gray node means a cycle. Test with a graph that has a cycle: 0->1, 1->2, 2->0, 2->3 (true), and one without: 0->1, 1->2, 2->3 (false).

Expected output:
true
false
challenges.hint: Use an array of colors (0=white, 1=gray, 2=black). In DFS, mark a node gray on entry. If you see a gray neighbor, there's a cycle. Mark black on exit.

steps.title: Three-color DFS scheme
steps.explanation: White (0) = unvisited, Gray (1) = currently being explored (on the recursion stack), Black (2) = fully explored. A back edge to a gray node proves a cycle exists in a directed graph.
steps.code: let mut color = vec![0u8; n];

steps.title: Recursive DFS with cycle detection
steps.explanation: Mark the current node gray. For each neighbor: if gray, we found a cycle (back edge). If white, recurse. After exploring all neighbors, mark the node black. This correctly handles disconnected components by starting DFS from every unvisited node.

steps.title: Handle disconnected components
steps.explanation: The outer loop starts DFS from each unvisited (white) node, ensuring cycles in disconnected components are also detected.

challenges.title: Expr Evaluate
challenges.description:
Write `evaluate(expr: &str) -> i64` that evaluates a simple arithmetic expression containing non-negative integers, +, -, and parentheses. Use a stack-based approach. Examples: "3+(2-1)" = 4, "10-(2+3-(1+1))" = 7. Print both results.

Expected output:
4
7
challenges.hint: Maintain a running result and a sign. When you hit '(', push the current result and sign onto a stack. When you hit ')', pop and combine. Parse multi-digit numbers by accumulating digits.

steps.title: Parse numbers and operators
steps.explanation: Iterate through characters. Accumulate consecutive digits into a number. On + or -, update the sign. Apply sign * num to the running result each time a complete number is parsed.

steps.title: Handle parentheses with a stack
steps.explanation: On '(', push the current (result, sign) and reset them — starting a fresh sub-expression. On ')', pop the saved state and combine: result = prev_result + prev_sign * current_result. This correctly handles nested parentheses.

steps.title: Why this works for nested expressions
steps.explanation: Each '(' creates a new scope by saving state to the stack. Nested parentheses simply add more frames. When ')' is encountered, we collapse the innermost scope back into its parent. This is elegant because there's no recursion — just a stack.

challenges.title: Knapsack Pack
challenges.description:
Implement `knapsack(weights: &[usize], values: &[usize], capacity: usize) -> usize` for the 0/1 knapsack problem using bottom-up DP. Each item can be taken at most once. For weights=[1,2,3], values=[6,10,12], capacity=5, the max value is 22 (items 2 and 3). For weights=[2,3,4,5], values=[3,4,5,6], capacity=5, the max value is 7.

Expected output:

steps.title: Space-optimized 1D DP
steps.explanation: Instead of a 2D table [items][capacity], use a 1D array of size capacity+1. dp[w] represents the maximum value achievable with capacity w. This reduces space from O(n*W) to O(W).
steps.code: let mut dp = vec![0usize; capacity + 1];

steps.title: Iterate in reverse to prevent reuse
steps.explanation: For each item, iterate capacity from high to low. This ensures each item is considered only once (0/1 constraint). If we iterated forward, dp[w - weight] might already include the current item, effectively allowing unlimited reuse.

steps.title: Read the answer
steps.explanation: dp[capacity] holds the maximum value achievable using any subset of items within the given capacity. The reverse iteration guarantees correctness of the 0/1 constraint.
steps.code: dp[capacity]

challenges.title: Deref Magic
challenges.description:
Create a custom smart pointer `MyBox<T>` that wraps a value and implements the `Deref` trait to allow transparent access to the inner value. Implement `MyBox::new(x: T)` and `Deref` for `MyBox<T>`. In main, create a MyBox<String> holding "hello world", then call a function `print_len(s: &str)` that prints the length — demonstrating Deref coercion from &MyBox<String> -> &String -> &str. Also print the value via *my_box.

Expected output:
11
hello world
challenges.hint: Deref's deref() method returns &self.value. Rust's Deref coercion will automatically chain: &MyBox<String> -> &String -> &str when needed.

steps.title: Define the smart pointer struct
steps.explanation: MyBox<T> is a generic struct that owns a value of type T. This mimics how Box<T> works, but on the stack. The new() method simply wraps the value.

steps.title: Implement Deref trait
steps.explanation: The Deref trait has an associated type Target = T and requires fn deref(&self) -> &T. By returning &self.value, the * operator and automatic Deref coercion work seamlessly.

steps.title: Deref coercion in action
steps.explanation: When print_len(&my_box) is called, Rust automatically applies Deref coercion: &MyBox<String> -> &String (via our Deref impl) -> &str (via String's Deref impl). This chain happens implicitly and is one of Rust's most powerful ergonomic features.
steps.code: print_len(&my_box);

challenges.title: Custom Serialize
challenges.description:
Define a `User` struct with fields name (String), age (u32), and active (bool). Implement a `serialize(&self) -> String` method that produces the format "name:age:active". Also implement `deserialize(s: &str) -> Option<User>` that parses this format back. In main, serialize a user, print it, then deserialize it back and print the fields.

Expected output:
Alice:30:true
name=Alice, age=30, active=true
challenges.hint: For serialize, use format!() with colon separators. For deserialize, split by ':', parse each field, and return None if parsing fails.

steps.title: Implement serialize with format!
steps.explanation: Use Rust's format! macro to join fields with a colon delimiter. The Display trait on String, u32, and bool handles conversion automatically.

steps.title: Implement deserialize with parsing
steps.explanation: Split the string by ':', validate we have exactly 3 parts, then parse each field. Use the ? operator on .ok() to convert parse errors into None, making the function return Option<User> cleanly.

steps.title: Round-trip test
steps.explanation: Serializing then deserializing should produce identical data. The .ok()? pattern converts Result to Option, propagating None on any parse failure — a common Rust idiom for fallible parsing.

challenges.title: State Machine
challenges.description:
Model a turnstile state machine with enums. Define `enum State { Locked, Unlocked }` and `enum Event { Coin, Push }`. Implement `fn transition(state: State, event: Event) -> State` using match. Rules: Locked+Coin->Unlocked, Locked+Push->Locked, Unlocked+Coin->Unlocked, Unlocked+Push->Locked. In main, start from Locked and apply events [Coin, Push, Push, Coin, Coin, Push], printing the state after each.

Expected output:
Unlocked
Locked
Locked
Unlocked
Unlocked
Locked
challenges.hint: Use a nested match: match (state, event) with four arms covering all combinations of State and Event.

steps.title: Model states and events as enums
steps.explanation: Enums are perfect for state machines in Rust because the compiler ensures exhaustive matching — you can't forget a state/event combination. Derive Debug for printing and Clone so state can be moved through transitions.

steps.title: Implement transitions with tuple matching
steps.explanation: Match on (state, event) tuples to handle all four combinations. The compiler guarantees exhaustiveness — if you add a new State or Event variant later, Rust will force you to handle it.

steps.title: Drive the state machine
steps.explanation: Process a sequence of events by folding over them, updating state at each step. Printing after each transition shows the machine's behavior. This pattern scales well — add more states/events and Rust ensures correctness at compile time.
